title: SEGMENT 4 — Keasling's artemisinin: the prototype modern metabolic engineering project

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Keasling's artemisinin: the prototype modern metabolic engineering project
Let me tell you about the project that, more than any other single demonstration, made metabolic engineering famous outside its own discipline.
The project is the engineering of yeast to produce artemisinic acid, a precursor to the antimalarial drug artemisinin.
The lead investigator is Jay Keasling, then at UC Berkeley.
The funder is the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation.
The commercial partner is Sanofi.
The work spans roughly a decade, from the early 2000s to the early 2010s.
And it is, by general agreement, the prototype of what modern, ambitious metabolic engineering looks like.
The motivating problem is malaria.
Malaria kills hundreds of thousands of people a year, mostly children, mostly in sub-Saharan Africa.
The most effective drug class for treating malaria is the artemisinins, derived from a Chinese herb called Artemisia annua, sweet wormwood.
The active molecule, artemisinin, is a sesquiterpene lactone with an unusual peroxide bridge that makes it lethal to the malaria parasite.
It is extracted from the plant.
The plant is grown in fields, primarily in China and Vietnam, harvested, and the artemisinin is purified.
The problem is supply.
The plant takes about eight months to grow.
Yields are variable, weather-dependent, subject to political and agricultural disruption.
Prices spike and crash unpredictably.
Sometimes there is too little artemisinin and patients die.
Sometimes there is too much and farmers go broke.
Neither scenario is acceptable for a life-saving drug.
There needed to be a stable, scalable, microbial source.

Keasling's lab proposed to engineer yeast to produce artemisinic acid — the chemical immediate-precursor of artemisinin — and then to use chemical synthesis to finish the conversion from artemisinic acid to artemisinin.
This split — biological synthesis of the hard precursor, chemical conversion of the easy final step — is called a semi-synthetic process, and it is a common pattern in metabolic engineering.
You let the cell do the part the cell is good at, which is the hard, stereochemically complex biosynthesis, and you let chemistry handle the simpler oxidations and rearrangements at the end.
The combined process can be cheaper and more reliable than either approach alone.
Now the actual engineering.
Artemisinic acid is a terpenoid, which means it belongs to a huge class of natural products built up from five-carbon isoprene units.

Yeast naturally has a terpenoid pathway — the mevalonate pathway — that produces a building block called farnesyl pyrophosphate, or FPP, a fifteen-carbon terpenoid intermediate that the cell normally uses to make ergosterol, its membrane sterol.
Farnesyl pyrophosphate is, conveniently, the right starting point for artemisinic acid.
So the strategy was: take yeast, push more carbon flux through its native mevalonate pathway to overproduce FPP, then install the plant enzymes that convert FPP into artemisinic acid.
Pushing flux through mevalonate meant overexpressing the rate-limiting enzymes — particularly HMG-CoA reductase, the famous statin target — to lift the bottleneck.

It also meant downregulating the squalene synthase that pulls FPP off into ergosterol synthesis, so that more FPP would accumulate available for the new pathway rather than being siphoned away to membrane lipids.
So you have one set of changes that lifts the upstream flow and another set that closes off the downstream drain, both pushing more FPP into the pool.
This kind of paired push-pull strategy is utterly characteristic of metabolic engineering.
Then the new pathway.
They found, in Artemisia annua, three enzymes that take FPP through the conversion to artemisinic acid.
Amorphadiene synthase cyclizes FPP into a hydrocarbon called amorphadiene.
CYP71AV1, a cytochrome P450 enzyme from the plant, oxidizes amorphadiene through several steps to artemisinic acid.
There is also a partner reductase needed to keep the P450 supplied with electrons.

Each of these enzymes was cloned from Artemisia annua, the genes were codon-optimized for yeast, put under strong yeast promoters, integrated into the yeast chromosome, and the engineered yeast was grown in a fermenter on sugar.

Out the back came artemisinic acid — at first in tiny amounts, then, through ten years of iterative engineering, in commercially relevant quantities, hitting yields of more than 25 grams per liter in the final published strain.
The downstream story is just as important as the engineering.
The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, which had been investing heavily in malaria treatment, funded the project.
The technology was developed at the not-for-profit Institute for OneWorld Health, with the explicit social goal that the resulting drug would be affordable in malaria-endemic countries.

The completed strain and process were transferred — royalty-free for malaria applications — to Sanofi, the French pharmaceutical company, which built out commercial-scale fermentation facilities, took the yeast-produced artemisinic acid through the chemical conversion to artemisinin, and put the resulting drug into the global supply chain in 2014.
For several years, Sanofi-manufactured semi-synthetic artemisinin was a substantial source of supply for the World Health Organization's malaria programs.
The economics ended up complicated — agricultural artemisinin remained cheaper at times, and Sanofi eventually scaled back the program — but the proof of principle is undisputed.

You could take a plant natural product, install its pathway in yeast, hit industrial yields, transfer the technology to a major pharmaceutical company, and feed the resulting drug into a life-saving global supply chain.
The whole thing worked.

The analogy I want you to carry: the Keasling artemisinin yeast is the prototype modern metabolic engineering project — full pathway from yeast precursor to antimalarial precursor, semi-synthetic completion, Gates-funded, technology transferred to Sanofi, drug in the field.

It is the demonstration that this discipline, the way the genetic circuits people demonstrated their field with the toggle switch and the repressilator, can do something at industrial scale that is socially important.
After artemisinin, every funding agency and every venture capital firm interested in biology paid attention to metabolic engineering.
The field went from a research subspecialty to a recognized arena for serious commercial and humanitarian investment.
We will, in later segments, see how the wave that followed broke into biofuels, fragrances, food proteins, and a dozen other product categories.
But before we go to those waves, we need to talk about the central engineering problem you have to solve to do any of this.
You cannot install a pathway without knowing what the pathway is.
You have to find the enzymes.
That is a problem in itself, and it is what we turn to next.
